figures.caption: Fig. 1. Comparison of Ada3D with several convolutional paradigms, including standard 2D convolution (Conv2D), adaptive 2D convolution (Ada2D), standard 3D convolution (Conv3D), depth-wise 3D convolution (DW3D), grouped 3D PAC (GPAC3D) [1], D2Conv3D [2], and ST-A3DNet [3]. For fairness, all methods have an equal number of parameters. Quantitative evaluation outcomes on the WDC dataset demonstrates the superior effectiveness and efficiency of Ada3D. For detailed results, please refer to Table VI.

Fig. 5. The Ada3D block. Taking Fa and Fb as inputs, the kernel and bias generators produce adaptive kernels K and biases D. The acquired K and D are then applied to Fb, facilitating the integration of spatial and spectral information. The 1D and 2D convolutional layers use kernel sizes of 3 and 3× 3, both with a stride and padding of 1. Normalization is applied over each k × k × k kernel field, and the number of hidden channels is determined by α and β.

Fig. 10. Ablation study on three hyper-parameters: block number, α, and β. The first row corresponds to the hyper-spectral pansharpening task, evaluated on the testing samples of the WDC dataset, while the second row pertains to the pansharpening task, evaluated on the reduced-resolution testing samples of the WV3 dataset. The black dots represent our chosen settings.

TABLE II QUANTITATIVE EVALUATION RESULTS ON TESTING SAMPLES OF THE WDC, BOTSWANA, AND PAVIA DATASETS, WHICH BELONG TO THE HYPER-SPECTRAL PANSHARPENING TASK. THE BEST RESULTS ARE HIGHLIGHTED IN BOLD, WHILE THE SECOND-BEST RESULTS ARE UNDERLINED. METHODS LISTED ABOVE THE DIVIDING LINE REPRESENT TRADITIONAL APPROACHES, WHEREAS THOSE BELOW IT ARE DL-BASED TECHNIQUES.

Fig. 8. Qualitative evaluation results on a reduced-resolution example from the WV3 dataset, which belongs to the pansharpening task. Rows 1 and 3: Pseudo-color images representing spectral bands 1, 3, and 5. Rows 2 and 4: The corresponding AEMs for spectral band 7.

Fig. 2. Left: A visual representation of how 2D modeling introduces spectral distortions. (a) The vector space of spectral data, where the number of spectral bands L is set to 3 for simplicity. (b) The vector space of spectral information, which is encoded into feature map channels via a convolutional layer. This scenario illustrates the case where rank(A) = 2 < L, resulting in a loss of spectral information. Right: A comparison of spectral distortions between Ada3D and SOTA 2D methods for image fusion, including Pan-Mamba [46], HyperDSNet [47], and U2Net [25]. Spectral distortions are quantified utilizing the Dλ [48] index, with lower Dλ values indicating fewer spectral distortions. Notably, Ada3D exhibits the lowest spectral distortions across all tested datasets.

Fig. 11. Qualitative evaluation results of SOTA HISR methods on two testing samples from the CAVE dataset. Row 1: Pseudo-color images of spectral bands 6, 10, and 26 for the testing example cd. Row 2: AEMs of spectral band 26 for cd. Row 3: Pseudo-color images of spectral bands 6, 13, and 26 for the testing example bears. Row 4: AEMs of spectral band 20 for bears.

TABLE III QUANTITATIVE EVALUATION RESULTS ON 20 REDUCED-RESOLUTION AND 20 FULL-RESOLUTION SAMPLES FROM THE WV3 DATASET, WHICH BELONGS TO THE PANSHARPENING TASK. THE SYMBOL ∗ REPRESENTS DL-BASED METHODS THAT UTILIZE ADAPTIVE 2D CONVOLUTIONS.

Fig. 4. Graphical illustration of our network architecture, which consists of two main sections: a spatial branch and a spectral branch. The former focuses on extracting spatial details, while the latter captures spectral characteristics and progressively achieves information integration using Ada3D blocks.
